blocks.text: This letter confirms that {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} has engaged Ashcombe Advisory Partners to design and run an investor relations program over the coming months. It sets out the work we will do, the people who will do it, what it costs, and the terms we both agree to. If any part of it does not match the conversation we had, tell us before you sign rather than after. An investor relations program only works when the firm and the company are describing the same business to the market, and this letter is the first place we get to confirm that we are.

The engagement is an investor relations program: the ongoing work of explaining {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} to the shareholders, analysts, and prospective investors whose understanding of the company moves its standing in the market. We will settle the core messages that carry the company's story, prepare the material that supports each earnings and announcement cycle, and stay close through the moments when a number has to be explained or a decision made public. We are not there to run your investor relations in perpetuity; we are there to build the program, the language, and the discipline so that the story holds the same shape each time it is told.

Our approach is plain and we have used it for years. We begin by listening, to you and to the people inside Castaneda, Adams and Davis who own the numbers and the narrative, and to the outside audiences who already hold a view we may need to sharpen or correct. From there we agree a short set of core messages in writing, because a position that lives only in conversation drifts the moment three people repeat it. We then build the materials that carry those messages through the calendar ahead, and we would rather say less and have it hold than say more and spend the next quarter walking it back.

Michael Morris will lead the engagement day to day and is your point of contact for anything of substance. He carries our more sensitive accounts and is at his best in the weeks before an announcement, when a clear head is worth more than a clever line. Laura Bradley will draft much of the client-facing messaging; she matches her register to the audience without losing the point, and she is quick to flag the sentence that could be read the wrong way. Michelle Black will prepare the underlying material each message rests on, keeping what is confirmed cleanly apart from what is still open. I lead this firm and I read the work that leaves it. I will not sit on your calls week to week, but I am reachable directly, and if the program is not going the way you expected I would rather hear it from you early than read it later.

Our fee for this engagement is {{fact:f:E-2018-001.fee}}, fixed at the outset and quoted as a single figure rather than as a rate against hours. We work to a fixed fee because it keeps our attention on the outcome instead of the clock, and because you should be able to call us with a question without watching a meter run. The fee is billed in equal monthly installments across the term, the first on commencement. Reasonable out-of-pocket costs that you approve in advance, such as travel to meet your investors or the production of finished materials, are billed at cost and itemized on each invoice. Invoices are payable within thirty days of receipt.

The engagement commences on {{fact:f:E-2018-001.start}} and runs approximately nine months, which is the basis on which we have planned the program. Either of us may end it on thirty days' written notice. If you end it, you owe the installments that have fallen due as of that date and the approved costs incurred to it, and nothing further. If we end it, which we would do only if the work as agreed had become work we could not do honestly, we will refund any fee already paid for work not yet performed.

Discretion is central to this kind of work. We will treat your internal information, your plans, and the fact and content of this engagement as confidential, and we will use anything you tell us about {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} that is not already public only to do the work you have retained us for, sharing it no further than the people on both sides who need it. Where a disclosure carries its own legal timing, we follow yours, and we will not release, hint at, or act on material information ahead of the company. We ask the same discretion of you regarding our methods and our materials.

We will carry out this engagement with the care and skill customary in our profession. We do not control how an audience receives a message or how a market moves on a given day, and we do not warrant a particular share price, analyst rating, or outcome, because the parts that decide those are not ours to command. Our total liability under this engagement, on any theory, is limited to the fees actually paid to us for it, and neither of us is liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss. Nothing in this paragraph limits liability for fraud or willful misconduct, and nothing should.

This letter is the entire agreement between us on this engagement and replaces anything said or drafted before it. It is governed by the law of the state in which this firm keeps its principal office, and any dispute we cannot settle between ourselves goes to the courts sitting in that state, to whose jurisdiction we each consent. Any amendment takes effect only in writing signed by both of us.

If this letter reflects our agreement, please sign below and return one copy to me. Michael Morris will be in touch within the week to set the kickoff and to agree the first pieces of work with your team at Castaneda, Adams and Davis. We are glad you chose us for this program. It is good, winnable work, and we intend to earn the choice.

We are underway on the investor relations program for {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}}, which commences on {{fact:f:E-2018-001.start}}. Our client contact is Lauren Heath, Chief Operating Officer at Castaneda, Adams and Davis, and her sign-off is the one we route to. The object of the program is plain: a clear, consistent story for the company in front of its investors, and the discipline to keep telling it the same way through the quarters ahead. This memo is here to get us pointed the same direction before the work spreads out. We are at the very start, so nothing below is fixed; treat it as the plan of record until we change it.

A word on how I want this one run, because the value of an investor relations program leaks out through small inconsistencies more than through any single misstep. A figure framed one way in a script and another way in a Q&A is exactly the kind of thing an analyst remembers, and remembers out loud. We keep one version of the story, we agree it in writing, and we do not let a helpful edit in one corner quietly contradict a line already cleared in another. When something is genuinely a judgment call, I would rather we lay out the two clean options and pick one than leave it open and hope it resolves itself. We have a full program ahead of us, so the early weeks matter more than they look; the habits we set now are the ones we will be living with in the quarters that count.

items: Messaging framework. Laura Bradley owns the first draft: a short set of core messages for the {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} story, agreed in writing and built off the kickoff conversation. It is the spine everything else hangs from, so it goes first.

Supporting material. Michelle Black will assemble the facts and proof points behind each message, keeping what is confirmed cleanly apart from what is still open, so a draft can be built without guessing at a number.
Investor calendar. A working schedule of the earnings and announcement moments ahead, held loosely for now because the dates will move. We align to the company's timing, never to ours.
Client cadence. A simple rhythm of status to Lauren Heath so Castaneda, Adams and Davis is never learning of a change after the fact. Every contact point with her gets filed in one place.

blocks.text: We will hold a short internal check each week to keep the workstreams honest against each other, and I will carry a plain status to Lauren Heath on the same rhythm so the client is never learning of a change after the fact. Anything that slips, flag it early. A problem raised on the day it appears is a scheduling note; the same problem raised a week later is an apology. If you are unsure whether something is settled, assume it is not and ask, because that is cheaper every time than unwinding it after it has gone out.

items: Laura Bradley to circulate the first messaging draft for internal review before it goes anywhere near the client.
Michelle Black to open the fact file, source each proof point, and flag any figure we cannot yet stand behind.
I will set the first check-in with Lauren Heath, agree the review rhythm with her, and keep her warm in the meantime.

blocks.text: Keep the client-facing channel tight: anything going to Lauren Heath or anyone else at Castaneda, Adams and Davis comes through me first. Thanks, both. This is a good program to get right.

To: Lauren Heath
Cc: Laura Bradley, Michelle Black

A short update on where the investor relations program for {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} stands, and one thing we could use from your side. We are still early, but the shape of the work is set and the pieces are moving in the right order. Nothing here needs action today; I mainly want you to be looking at the same picture we are.

Laura Bradley has the first cut of the core messaging drafted off the kickoff conversation, and Michelle Black is assembling the proof points behind each line so nothing in the story rests on a figure we cannot stand behind. Our plan is to get the messaging framework to you next week for a first read, well before any of it points toward an announcement. I would rather you push back on the language now, when it costs a redraft, than after it has traveled.

The one ask for now is thirty minutes with you and whoever owns the numbers on the Castaneda, Adams and Davis side, so we hear the investor concerns in your own words before the language hardens. Send me a couple of windows that work this week or next and I will set it up.
Prepared by Ashcombe Advisory Partners for the leadership of Castaneda, Adams and Davis. An early briefing on the program that commenced {{fact:f:E-2018-001.start}} for {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}}.

items: The core messaging framework is drafted and in internal review.
The proof points behind each message are being confirmed one by one.
The investor calendar for the quarters ahead is mapped in draft.
We are in the early phase: settling the story before it travels.
The aim this quarter is a story that holds under a hard question.
Michael Morris, Partner -- engagement lead and your point of contact.
Laura Bradley, Senior Associate -- messaging and client-facing drafts.
Michelle Black, Communications Analyst -- supporting material and proof points.
Lauren Heath, Chief Operating Officer -- program sponsor at Castaneda, Adams and Davis.
A short set of core messages that carry the company's story.
Each claim sitting over a proof point we can stand behind.
One version of the story, told the same way to every audience.
Language tuned for investors and tested against the hard question.
What is confirmed kept cleanly apart from what is still open.
Earnings and announcement moments sequenced across the term.
Timing aligned to the company's schedule, never to ours.
A single working calendar kept current so no one is guessing.
Decisions on order made together, and made early rather than late.
Finalize the messaging framework and circulate the clean version.
Walk the framework with the {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} leadership for sign-off.
Prepare the material for the first investor cycle under the new frame.

blocks.text: Meeting Minutes: Investor Relations Program for {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}}

items: Michael Morris, Partner
Laura Bradley, Senior Associate
Michelle Black, Communications Analyst
Lauren Heath, Chief Operating Officer, Castaneda, Adams and Davis

blocks.text: The session took stock of the investor relations program at roughly its midpoint and was called to settle the messaging framework and line up the work of the next phase. Michael Morris opened by walking the group through the framework, now largely settled after the last round of internal review. Mike's read was that the core story holds, and that the work from here is to pressure-test it against the harder investor questions rather than to keep reworking the language. He asked that the group treat the framework as closed unless a real problem surfaces, so the effort moves outward rather than circling the same paragraphs.

Laura Bradley reviewed the framework claim by claim. Most of the supporting lines are confirmed; two items on the operations side are still open, and Lauren Heath agreed to route the missing figures from the Castaneda, Adams and Davis team by the end of the week. Michelle Black noted where each proof point currently sits and flagged the one claim that cannot yet be sourced, so it is held out of any draft until it can be. The group agreed a claim without a confirmed proof point does not travel, however good it reads, and that the fact file is the single record of what is settled.

Discussion then turned to the investor calendar and the risk of drift. Lauren Heath raised the concern that the internal and external language stay in step as leadership begins using the new framing in day-to-day conversation, and asked how the firm would catch small variations before they harden. Michael Morris proposed that the weekly status thread carry any change to a cleared line, so there is one place to look. The group agreed to keep that thread running and to reconvene for a full working session once the first investor-facing materials are drafted. Any interim decision is to be confirmed in writing on the thread so nothing turns on a hallway conversation.

rows: Finalize the messaging framework and circulate the clean version
Confirm the two open operations proof points and update the fact file
Route the missing operations figures from the client side
Sequence the first investor-facing materials against the frame
Log any change to a cleared line on the weekly status thread

blocks.text: Prepared for the leadership of {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} by Michael Morris, Partner, Ashcombe Advisory Partners. This report covers the program through the current period, at roughly the halfway mark of the term.

The program is on plan. The core messaging framework is settled and in use internally, the supporting proof points are largely confirmed, and we are now moving from building the story to putting it to work in front of investors. Nothing on the timeline has slipped in a way that concerns us, and the few open items are the ordinary loose ends of a program at its midpoint rather than warning signs. The shape of the second half is straightforward: less writing, more testing, and the disciplined work of keeping one version of the story as more people begin to tell it. Where a judgment call is coming, this report names it plainly so there are no surprises later, which is the way we prefer to run an account and the way we said we would run this one.

Laura Bradley finalized the messaging framework and circulated the clean version for leadership review. The core statement is tighter than the first draft, each claim now sits over a confirmed proof point, and the two lines that read as too promotional for an investor audience have been pulled. Michelle Black built out the supporting fact library behind the framework, keeping what is confirmed cleanly separate from what is still open, so that any draft that goes out rests only on figures the company can stand behind. Two open items remain on the operations side; the client has agreed to route the missing figures, and neither holds up the framework as a whole. Together those two pieces give us a story that is both consistent and defensible, which is the whole point of the exercise at this stage and the footing everything in the second half is built on.

Against the program budget of {{fact:f:E-2018-001.fee}}, we are roughly half spent at the halfway point, which tracks the plan closely. The fixed fee keeps our attention on the outcome rather than the clock, and we do not expect to come back for more; the remaining budget covers the investor-facing work still ahead, which is the heavier half by effort even if not by fee. Any out-of-pocket costs continue to be approved in advance and itemized, and there are none of note this period. If anything on the horizon looked likely to change that picture, this is where you would read about it first.

The next phase moves the framework outward. We will prepare the first investor-facing materials under the new frame, ready the earnings and announcement language for the cycles ahead, and rehearse the harder questions the framing invites, because the moment to find the weak answer is in a rehearsal and not on a call. Laura Bradley will lead the drafting and Michelle Black will keep the proof library current against it, so that as the calendar fills, every piece that leaves the firm for the market has been checked against the same settled story. We will also begin sequencing the announcement calendar in earnest, holding it against the company's timing rather than ours, and we will bring you a working version of that calendar at the next review so the decisions about order are made together and in good time rather than under pressure.

Two things are worth watching. The first is the drift that creeps in once a framework meets daily use: as the Castaneda, Adams and Davis team begins speaking in the new language, small variations will appear, and our job is to catch them before they harden into two versions of the story. The second is timing. Several announcement windows ahead are not fully in the company's control, and if two compress into the same stretch we will need to make a call on sequence quickly. Neither is a problem today; both are the kind of thing that is cheap to manage early and expensive to manage late, which is why they are on this page.
Prepared by Michael Morris, Partner, Ashcombe Advisory Partners.
